speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is May 5, 1995. This is Mr. Nathan Sobel. My name is Debbi Portnoy, Long Beach, New York, USA. Language spoken is English. My name is Debbi Portnoy, D-E-B-B-I P-O-R-T-N-O-Y. Today's date is May 18, 1995. I'm conducting an interview with Mr. Nathan Sobel in Long Beach, New York, United States of America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whenever you're ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell me your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Nathan Sobel, S-O-B-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how you spell your first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nathan, N-A-T-H-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a middle name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [HEBREW], which is similar to Nathan. If you read it, it's [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever called by any other name?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a little child, I was called by a couple of other names. I was called [HEBREW]. And the last name was Sojbel at that time, S-O-J-B-E-L. Subsequently, I changed it in Israel to S-O-I-B-E-L. When I got naturalized in the United States, I changed it to S-O-B-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: January 23, 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you now?

speaker: interviewer
text: What country were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's called Luboml, L-U-B-O-M-L. In Yiddish, we called it [YIDDISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have another name? A Polish name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish name and Russian name was Luboml, the same. But we Jews had our own names. For the town and for streets, we had our own separate naming of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Near which large city was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's between four larger cities. To the north was Brest-Litovsk, which is a Brisk. To the south, Volodymyr-Volynsk or [INAUDIBLE]. To the east was Kovel. To the west was Chelm. We were on the border on the Bug River, the border between Poland and Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your address?

speaker: interviewee
text: 51A [INAUDIBLE] Street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please describe Luboml. Was it a small town? A village?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a small town, what we called a shtetl, a marketplace. This town, I found out when I was in Israel, in the Biet Hatfutsot, the Museum of the Diaspora, they listed this town as one of the four oldest towns in Poland dating back to about 1000 to 1200.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jews lived in Luboml before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, maybe about 6,000, 7,000. At the war was about 10,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who lived in your household before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war was my-- my family, my father, mother, an older brother, my younger sister, and a younger brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Avraham Sojbel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Avram Leibish. And we called him Avram, Avrum.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was 1898. I do not know the-- the exact date.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a Hebrew date that you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was too young to know these things. And we didn't celebrate birthdays in Europe. And it was not the time. There was wars, and wars, so I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] or Bashie we called her. Bashie Rochel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have-- what was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maiden name was Faigen. F-A-I-G-E-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was 1906. And I don't know the date, either.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your oldest brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aaron-- Aaron Hirsch, Aaron Tzvi.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then the next brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my sister was after me. Her name was Sara. We called her [INAUDIBLE], Sara [INAUDIBLE], Sara Tova and my youngest brother Bentzion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anybody else that lived in the household with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was our-- our family. And it was considered in Europe, a small family. There's other people that had more children than we did. And we had a tenant before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The tenant's name, he was a Pole. His name was [INAUDIBLE]. And he plays later on in the story of survival. He has a role in one part, one point.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he married?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was married. He had a wife, and he had two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what the children's names were, the wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know the wife. She died subsequently. But he was a widower during the-- during the German occupation. And he lived with his older son. His name was Jurek.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of work did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a master mechanic. He-- when he was 12 years old-- that's how it was told to me-- when he was bar mitzvahed, he was already a year in business. He was making-- he was a locksmith. He was making locks and other mechanical work. Subsequently, he opened up a machine shop. He had motors and equipment. We would repair agriculture equipment and anything metal, anything that had to be used with a lathe, and millers, and drills, and so on, with motors, and so on. In the summer, the yard would be with 50, 60 different motors and other equipment. Subsequently, we also had a-- we-- my father had a dealing with bicycles. He put together bicycles and also radios. We also opened up a-- we built a new store, which we never occupied, because the war broke out, which was supposed to be for agricultural equipment, radios, and bicycles, in the marketplace.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father have any other activities or hobbies that he was involved in?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a-- a natural inventor. He worked in this shop, machine shop, with his brothers. He had three brothers and a sister. And she would be a secretary sometimes. His oldest brother was Gershon, then was Zelig, and Mershel, and Iossel. He had four brothers. And they would work with him in-- in the machine shop. And my father, I was told that he was a-- he was a quiet man, but he was a genius. At that time we didn't have electricity, so he made a battery. And we had electricity from the battery. Then he-- we didn't-- he didn't want to-- when electricity came in sometime in the '20s, he made an invention where the-- where the light would be connected to a certain central switch. And that switch would be connected to a wire down to where my-- my mother would light the candles. And with a thread, he would put a candle on top of it. When the candle burned out, which means around 11:00, 11:30 at night, the candle would light the wire, and the weight would pull the-- the wire, the whole thing, it would shut off the switch. Then he improved on it. And he made a-- he connected it with a grandfather clock. So he could pinpoint to the minute when he wanted the electricity to go off before Shabbos, because he was orthodox. He at one time, he also made scale that you weigh tons. But he made it so fine, the fulcrum was so fine, that if you put a feather on it, it would-- it was sensitive enough to move the scale. He also made master keys and other things. He always would create something and invent things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother work?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was house-- house-- a homemaker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have any activities or organizations that she belonged to, that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: When she was younger, I was told that a-- she would sing and dance. She was very well-known in-- in town. She would dance with a cane. She was a young-- young woman in her teens. And she was very artistic. But later on, her entire family, my mother's side, her mother and-- her mother, brother, and sister went to the United States. An older brother was in Russia. A younger brother went to Argentina. And she was the only one that was stranded in Poland. My father's entire family, on the other hand, was all in Luboml, in our town. So she was a homemaker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any type of Jewish youth organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was a kid. I was a child. And it was not as developed as now. But we were-- we were involved in Jewish and Zionist organizations. And when-- when I start-- when I was age two or three, I went to cheder, then to a Hebrew school. We studied Hebrew. And then we went to public school. We went to public school in the morning and Hebrew in the afternoon or evenings. So we were involved in different organizations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the cheder.

speaker: interviewee
text: The cheder was a place where you-- where children of your age come to a teacher. And he teaches you the alphabet. Subsequently, he teaches you to pray, and the Bible, and so on. When you reach the level of the Bible, or Rashi, you go to a higher teacher, a teacher who is more equipped or knows more to teach you the-- the Chumash, Rashi, which is the Bible. And Rashi is a-- is a-- a translation. It's an interpretation of the Bible. And then Gemara, but I was too young for the Gemara, I started the Gemara. But we also studied Hebrew, modern Hebrew. We would write poems in Hebrew and Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you were attending cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know since I was about three years old, maybe two or three.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was somewheres in the early '30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you ever involved in-- in Betar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: My family-- my town had all the organizations. There were all kinds of Hasidim in town, about 12 or 13 different Hasidic movements. But the younger people already belonged to different Zionist organizations. There was-- there was the Betar, and the HeHalutz, and the [INAUDIBLE], and the-- and other organizations. And my family belonged to the Betar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which Hasidic organizations were from the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], and the-- our town was the seat of the [SPEAKING HEBREW] rabbi, whose name was Twerski, Baruch Twerski. And his son-in-law was Moishele-- I forgot his name. It just escaped me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your religious background. Was it orthodox? Traditional?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Europe, we were basically traditional. We were all kosher. We-- my father didn't work Saturday. He would go to a synagogue. And we would go along with him. And would we-- we would eat only kosher. My grandfathers had beards. And both grandfathers were rabbis. One grandfather, that I'm named after, was a teacher. Was a [HEBREW] a teacher in a cheder. My other grandfather was a [HEBREW] for-- for a-- for a rabbi. And he had a whole [HEBREW] in his house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember which rabbi gave him [HEBREW]?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to go back one second to you had mentioned that you went to a Polish school. What was the name of the Polish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our town, they had three schools. There was a Polish school. They were all government schools, but the Polish school, only Polish children went. The-- there was a Jewish school where Jewish children went. Then there was a Ukrainian school, where the Ukrainian children went. And I went to the Jewish school, which was called [HEBREW], which means public school number two in-- in there. But the basic language was Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where the school was located, or the address, or what street?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was located on the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in a place called the [HEBREW]. The-- the fire department was downstairs. And we had the school upstairs. Subsequent to that, we went to a school called [HEBREW], which was a division where children third or fourth grade would go there before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe some of the religious rituals that you might have kept in your household?

speaker: interviewee
text: Religious rituals. When it came Shabbos, on Saturdays, my mother prepared for Shabbos like every household. They would cook, and bake, and prepare, and give to the poor. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we would have candles, Shabbos candles. We would have [HEBREW]. We would have Hanukkah candles. We would have-- such a long time ago. We would have all the Jewish objects that we had-- that a Jewish home would have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which synagogue did your family go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father went to the [HEBREW]. It was a little shul that belonged to the [HEBREW] rabbi, which he had the seat in Luboml, but he had another place where the main Hasidi went with the beards. This was for the more modern followers. They didn't have beards anymore, but they were Hasidic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where that was located?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was connected to the main synagogue, which I have photographs. I'll be able to point out that little [HEBREW].

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the main synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's called the [HEBREW], the great synagogue. That synagogue was unique in Poland. It was built around 600 or 700 years ago by some Italian architect. And it was built as a fortress. And whenever they write about synagogues in Europe, they point out the synagogue in Luboml, which existed all these years and survived of the pogroms, and all the wars, and all the-- and all the invasions, and till-- till this war. When after this war, they destroyed the synagogue. The synagogue had walls which was a meter and a half thick. It was very huge, but it was all destroyed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What languages did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home we spoke Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And outside, in the street, during everyday life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everyday, among ourselves, we would speak Yiddish. When we came to-- to the school, we had to speak-- we were forced to speak Polish. If we didn't speak Polish, we would have to pay fine of 2 [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How much was the fine?

speaker: interviewee
text: 2 [INAUDIBLE]. And for us children, it was a-- it was a lot of money. It meant not having an ice cream.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much is a-- is a [INAUDIBLE] in today's value?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was nothing. Really very, very, very little. But you can buy a candy. Kids measure in terms of an ice cream or a candy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the-- the rabbi of the synagogue? What was his name again?

speaker: interviewee
text: The [HEBREW] rabbi was, they called him the [HEBREW] rabbi. I have to look up for the name in the book. His name was Baruch Twerski. And he had a son-in-law, which was-- I think I gave you the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of child were you before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] was his name. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of a child where you before the war? Describe yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was kind-- I was very skinny. And I would eat very little. And I was more the studious type, scholarly type. I would not go into-- most kids would go into playing ball and other things. I would do it every once in a while. But I would make my own little notebook. And I would make little notes in it. Till today, I do this thing. I make a little note, I put it in my pocket. And I would always try to do something different or invent something. As a matter of fact, I have twenty some odd inventions and some patents, so does my son. He's the youngest inventor in the United States. But I would always go off on a side. I would have one friend. And he would also-- he was also a scholar like myself. And we would try to talk about physics and other things, what's happening in the world. We were little, but we-- we were interested in these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your friend's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moishe Blumin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please describe contact with non-Jews before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: We almost said no contact. Our town is-- had about 70% of the population, 70% of population was Jewish. And 30% were Poles and Ukrainians. Later on, they attached several villages as part of the town. Because in World War I, there was a Jewish man that was the mayor of the town, and they didn't like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had annexed several villages and made the town larger. So therefore, the Jewish population was diminished or reduced. And they were able to elect a Polish-- a Polish mayor and a Ukrainian vice mayor and a Jewish secretary. Did you personally have any anti-Semitic experiences before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, whenever it came around Christmas time. Specifically, when we would go to cheder or to Hebrew school late at night, the days were short. And when we finished school, we would go to Hebrew. And we would go home with lanterns to see our way-- which we didn't have exactly paved streets. It was all boards, and-- and-- and with snow. It would be wet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they would attack us with stones and sticks. They would throw stones at us and attack us, beat us up. I-- I sustained once, a stone. Tell me what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened? I-- we-- we ran for our lives. We were little, and they were bigger than us. And they would know when we would go. We would go, we'd bunch up a few kids together with a couple of lanterns. We would go home. Some people would go in different directions. And when we were three or four kids together, they would throw stones at us or come with sticks, and we would run.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must have been six or seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year would that have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: This could have been somewheres in the '30s. It was the time when Hitler rose to power in Germany. At that point, the head of the Polish government, his name was Marshal Jozef Pilsudski, he died. And a new guy came, [INAUDIBLE]. He was more anti-Semitic. And they started taking away rights that the Jews had had before. And they started cutting out different things. And they became more anti-Semitic. So the-- the air-- the air was very bad. It got worse and worse. In the '30s, it was very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any type of pogroms or anything else that occurred before the war actually started?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not-- not in our town. We would just get-- I-- I understand that the adult population suffered. They would take away some of the businesses and give it to exclusively to Polish or Ukrainian people. And the Jews would be bought. We couldn't get out of town. Whoever left in the '20s or early '30s, they escaped, they went to the United States, to Argentina. Some went to Russia. Some went to Palestine. And others went to France, wherever they could. And later on, they shut all-- all our borders, and you couldn't get out. We were locked in. My--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family ever try to get out?

speaker: interviewee
text: We tried to get out. My father's sister was engaged to a-- to a man, and he went to Palestine. So then they had to pay somebody else. And they-- she was attached. They said she was his fictitious wife. And he took her out. She went with him to Palestine. The she married her actual husband in Palestine. This was in 1934, I believe, or '35. And my uncle, my mother's youngest brother, tried to come to the States. He had a brother, a sister, and mother in the United States. And they wouldn't give him any-- any-- he was a writer. And they wouldn't him any papers. They just denied. I believe it was in 1928.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who denied the papers? Was it the Polish government?

speaker: interviewee
text: The United States government. Poland still was willing to let him out, but the US government denied. And he went instead to Argentina, as a-- as a farmer, as a farm worker.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was somewheres in 1928.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your immediate family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My immediate family, my mother was trying to get papers from the United States. And they tried to make papers out for us to come. And we were already a family of five, six.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my aunt-- it was the depression in the United States, and they didn't have much money. They were in the States 15, 20 years, but they didn't have much money. And the US government demanded $3,000. The family had to show $3,000 for every person they would try to give a permit to to leave. And the entire family was able to put together only $3,000. So my father and mother said, either one person goes, and the rest stays. Since they were talking about war, they just decided to stay and not to leave anybody. So that's how we didn't go. 1937, 1938, we could have come here. But because of the limitation, we couldn't get out. How did your life begin to change when the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was in school in 1939. I remember instead of having classes, the teacher said all children must go out into the hallway. It was a big hallway, and they had one radio. In the corner of the room was hanging, a wooden radio. And we must listen to that special announcement. And we said it was kind of strange. We knew from the parents that things are happening in the world. And we heard that in Germany things are happening. They were throwing out Jews and shooting and killing. It was only 1939, I believe it was September first. And at that point, they started announcing that the German is demanding a part of Poland, and Poland wouldn't give in. And their big hero [INAUDIBLE] said no. And because of that, the Germans marched into Poland and the war broke out. And we didn't know what it was. The war broke out. And it happened that in one day he ran away from Poland and disappeared. And in 14 days the Germans crossed all of Poland, and 14 days after they were in our town. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning they marched in, and they opened up soup kitchens. They would give the soup to the tens of thousands of refugees who had-- who had run form west to east, towards Russia. They would cross our town. And my father was busy helping fix bicycles and fix cars, and fix wagons, and fix all kinds of things. And they would stream, tens of thousands of people, running, running east. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and-- and then they stayed for several days, and they were good. They did not bother us. They would give out food to-- to the hungry, to refugees, and war, prisoners of war, Polish prisoners of war. And then they kept saying, [NON-ENGLISH], here will be the-- the Russians. [NON-ENGLISH] is Russian. Says that they're going to leave, and the Russians will come instead. And we didn't understand. They say the Germans are bad. They don't look bad at all to us. But we stayed from far. But the Ukrainians came and welcomed them with flowers. And the girls smiled and sang and danced with them, the Ukrainians. But we kept away. We were told that they're not our friends. And they stayed for about a week. And then they retreated, they left the town. And the town remained without any military. So Ukrainians came running in from all over and started looting, shooting, killing. So the Jewish groups organized themselves, and they started protecting the town. And the-- some groups who were leftist built a gate to welcome the Russian, the Soviet Army. So at one point, the Polish soldiers which were running amok, they were running wild. They came to town, they saw the gate for the-- for the Russians, the welcoming gate. And they went through the town, and they shot every Jew they found. And they found-- they shot about to 10, 12 people. Fathers and sons, and people who put their heads to the window, they would just shoot them and kill them. So they made their little pogrom. And then as they came, they went out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not see, I just-- a friend-- the father of a friend was killed. They found him in the street. People would just be in the street, they would just shoot you down. And then they--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. But the book has them all listed, all the people. There was young people and older people. They would just shoot them down. And they would lie in the streets, and houses, and doorways. And-- and-- and then they ran and went back to the railroad station and ran out-- out of town.
